questions.q: A smooth atlas on $M$ is a collection of charts $\{(U_\alpha,\varphi_\alpha)\}$ covering $M$ such that:
choices: the transition maps $\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1}$ are $C^\infty$ diffeomorphisms between open subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$
questions.explain: Smoothness of transition maps between Euclidean patches is exactly what lifts calculus from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $M$. No disjointness, compactness, or linearity is required.

questions.q: On $S^2$ with the two-chart stereographic atlas from $N$ and $S$, the overlap $U_N\cap U_S$ is $S^2$ minus how many points?
questions.explain: The north chart misses $N$ and the south chart misses $S$, so the overlap is $S^2\setminus\{N,S\}$ — two missing points.

questions.q: The transition map between the two stereographic charts on $S^2$ is:
questions.explain: Composing the two stereographic projections gives $(u,v)\mapsto(u,v)/(u^2+v^2)$, a $C^\infty$ diffeomorphism of $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$.

hard.q: The circle $S^1$ admits a smooth atlas with two charts. Which statement is a correct counterexample showing that a single chart cannot cover $S^1$?
choices: A single chart would require a homeomorphism $S^1\to\mathbb{R}$, but $S^1$ is compact and $\mathbb{R}$ is not
A single chart is disallowed by the definition of manifold regardless of topology
No open subset of $\mathbb{R}$ can be diffeomorphic to an arc of $S^1$
The transition map of a single-chart atlas would not be smooth
hard.explain: A single chart would require a homeomorphism from all of $S^1$ onto an open subset of $\mathbb{R}$. But $S^1$ is compact and a continuous bijection from a compact space to a Hausdorff space is a homeomorphism onto its image; no open subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact, so no such homeomorphism exists.

hard.q: The torus $T^2 = S^1\times S^1$ carries a smooth atlas derived from the product of two two-chart atlases on $S^1$. What is the minimum number of charts in such a product atlas?
hard.explain: Each factor $S^1$ needs at least $2$ charts, so the product atlas has $2\times 2 = 4$ charts of the form $U_i\times V_j$.

hard.q: Which of the following is the correct order of steps in verifying that two charts $(U,\varphi)$ and $(V,\psi)$ are smoothly compatible?
choices: Check $U\cap V\ne\emptyset$, then verify $\psi\circ\varphi^{-1}$ is $C^\infty$ on $\varphi(U\cap V)$, then verify $\varphi\circ\psi^{-1}$ is $C^\infty$ on $\psi(U\cap V)$
Check $\varphi\circ\psi^{-1}$ is $C^\infty$, then verify $U$ and $V$ are disjoint
Verify $\varphi=\psi$ on $U\cap V$, then check surjectivity
Verify $U\cap V=\emptyset$, then the charts are automatically compatible
hard.explain: Two charts are smoothly compatible when their overlap is open (possibly empty) and both transition maps $\psi\circ\varphi^{-1}$ and $\varphi\circ\psi^{-1}$ are $C^\infty$. If the overlap is empty they are trivially compatible, skipping the smoothness check.

smooth-manifold-examples.title: Examples: spheres, projective spaces, matrix groups

questions.q: What is the dimension of the matrix Lie group $\mathrm{SO}(n)$?
questions.explain: $\mathrm{SO}(n)$ has dimension $n(n-1)/2$; for $n=3$ this is $3(3-1)/2=3$ — reflecting the three Euler angles.

questions.q: Real projective space $\mathbb{RP}^n$ can be described as:
choices: the quotient $S^n/\{\pm 1\}$ by the antipodal identification, an $n$-manifold
the complement of the origin in $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$
questions.explain: $\mathbb{RP}^n = S^n/\{\pm 1\}$ identifies antipodal points; the resulting space is a compact smooth $n$-manifold, with standard affine charts $\{x_i\ne 0\}$.

questions.q: The Grassmannian $\mathrm{Gr}(k,n)$ of $k$-planes in $\mathbb{R}^n$ has dimension $k(n-k)$. What is $\dim\mathrm{Gr}(2,5)$?
questions.explain: $\dim\mathrm{Gr}(2,5)=2\cdot(5-2)=6$. The charts are graphs of linear maps $W_0\to W_0^\perp$, giving $k(n-k)$ parameters.

hard.q: The unitary group $\mathrm{U}(n)$ is cut out of $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ by $A^*A=I$. What is its real dimension for $n=2$?
hard.explain: $\mathrm{U}(n)$ has real dimension $n^2$. For $n=2$ this gives $4$. (Concretely: $\mathrm{U}(2)\cong \mathrm{U}(1)\times\mathrm{SU}(2)/\mathbb{Z}_2$, with $\dim\mathrm{U}(1)=1$ and $\dim\mathrm{SU}(2)=3$.)

hard.q: Why is the cone $C=\{(x,y,z):z^2=x^2+y^2\}\subset\mathbb{R}^3$ NOT a smooth manifold near the apex $(0,0,0)$?
choices: The cone is not connected near the apex

Any neighbourhood of the apex in $C$ is not homeomorphic to an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^2$ — removing the apex disconnects it into two components, while removing a point from a $2$-disk leaves one component
The cone fails to be Hausdorff at the apex
Smoothness requires an analytic parametrization, which the cone does not admit
hard.explain: Take any small neighbourhood $U$ of the apex in $C$. Then $U\setminus\{0\}$ has two connected components (the two half-cones). But for any point in a smooth $2$-manifold, removing it leaves a connected punctured disk. So no such neighbourhood is homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^2$, and $C$ fails even to be a topological manifold at the apex — smoothness is not the issue, the topology is.

hard.q: Which of the following is a smooth manifold structure on the complex projective line $\mathbb{CP}^1$?
choices: $\mathbb{CP}^1\cong S^2$ as smooth manifolds, with two charts from the affine pieces $\{z_0\ne 0\}$ and $\{z_1\ne 0\}$ glued by $w=1/z$
$\mathbb{CP}^1\cong\mathbb{R}^2$ as smooth manifolds; a single global chart suffices
$\mathbb{CP}^1$ is not a smooth manifold; it has a singular point at infinity
hard.explain: $\mathbb{CP}^1$ has two affine charts $U_0=\{[z_0:z_1]:z_0\ne 0\}$ with coordinate $z=z_1/z_0$ and $U_1=\{z_1\ne 0\}$ with coordinate $w=z_0/z_1$; on the overlap $w=1/z$, smooth (in fact holomorphic). Topologically the result is $S^2$ — the Riemann sphere — and one can check the smooth structures match the standard stereographic atlas.

questions.q: A map $f\colon M\to N$ between smooth manifolds is smooth iff:
choices: for every chart $(U,\varphi)$ on $M$ and $(V,\psi)$ on $N$ with $f(U)\subseteq V$, the coordinate representative $\psi\circ f\circ\varphi^{-1}$ is $C^\infty$
$f$ is a homeomorphism onto its image
questions.explain: Smoothness of $f$ is smoothness of every coordinate representative $\hat f = \psi\circ f\circ\varphi^{-1}$, which is a map between open subsets of Euclidean space. Chart compatibility makes this property independent of chart choice.

questions.q: A diffeomorphism $f\colon M\to N$ is:
choices: a smooth bijection whose inverse is also smooth
questions.explain: Diffeomorphisms are the isomorphisms of the smooth category: smooth, bijective, with smooth inverse. A smooth bijection need not be a diffeomorphism — consider $x\mapsto x^3$ on $\mathbb{R}$, whose inverse is not smooth at $0$.

questions.q: Let $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(x,y)=(x+y,\,xy)$. At $p=(1,2)$ the Jacobian is $\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\ y&x\end{pmatrix}_{(1,2)} = \begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&1\end{pmatrix}$. What is the rank of $df_p$?
questions.explain: $\det\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&1\end{pmatrix} = 1-2 = -1\ne 0$, so the Jacobian is invertible and $\operatorname{rank}(df_p)=2$.

hard.q: The map $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^3$, is a smooth bijection. Why is it NOT a diffeomorphism?
choices: $f^{-1}(y)=y^{1/3}$ is not differentiable at $y=0$ (its derivative blows up), so $f^{-1}$ is not smooth there
hard.explain: $f$ is a smooth bijection, but $df_0 = 3x^2|_{x=0} = 0$ is non-invertible, and $f^{-1}(y)=y^{1/3}$ has $(f^{-1})'(y)=\tfrac{1}{3}y^{-2/3}$, which diverges as $y\to 0$. So $f^{-1}$ fails to be smooth (in fact even $C^1$) at $0$, and $f$ is not a diffeomorphism. The inverse function theorem obstruction: $df_p$ must be an isomorphism at every $p$.

hard.q: Which condition on a smooth map $f\colon M\to N$ is necessary AND sufficient for $f$ to be a local diffeomorphism at $p\in M$?
choices: $df_p\colon T_pM\to T_{f(p)}N$ is an isomorphism (in particular, $\dim M = \dim N$)
$f$ pulls back smooth functions to smooth functions
hard.explain: The inverse function theorem: $f$ is a local diffeomorphism at $p$ iff $df_p$ is a linear isomorphism, which forces $\dim M=\dim N$. Injectivity near $p$ is implied but not sufficient (think $x\mapsto x^3$). Every smooth map pulls back smooth functions.

hard.q: Let $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(x,y)=(e^x\cos y,\,e^x\sin y)$ (complex exponential). What is $|\det(df_p)|$ at the point $p=(0,0)$?
hard.explain: $df_p = \begin{pmatrix} e^x\cos y & -e^x\sin y \\ e^x\sin y & e^x\cos y\end{pmatrix}$, with determinant $e^{2x}$. At $(0,0)$ this is $1$. So $f$ is a local diffeomorphism everywhere, though not globally injective — $f$ is $2\pi$-periodic in $y$.

questions.q: Select every statement that is true of the tangent space $T_pM$ at a point $p$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$.
choices: Equivalently, $T_pM$ is the space of point-derivations $D\colon C^\infty_p(M)\to\mathbb{R}$ at $p$ — linear maps satisfying the Leibniz rule $D(fg)=D(f)g(p)+f(p)D(g)$.
$T_pM$ depends on the choice of chart: different charts give non-isomorphic tangent spaces.
The coordinate vectors $\partial/\partial x^1|_p,\dots,\partial/\partial x^n|_p$ in any chart form a basis of $T_pM$.
$T_pM$ is canonically a subset of $M$ containing $p$.
questions.hint: Three equivalent definitions of $T_pM$ — equivalence-classes of curves, point-derivations, chart-dependent components — all give the same $n$-dimensional vector space, basis-independent.
questions.explain: $T_pM$ is an intrinsic $n$-dimensional real vector space. The point-derivation definition is one of three standard equivalent ones (the others use velocities of curves through $p$, or component-tuples under chart-change). Coordinate vectors give a basis in any chart, but the space itself is canonical (chart-independent). $T_pM$ is not a subset of $M$ — it is a separate vector space attached to $p$.

questions.q: What is the dimension of the tangent bundle $TM$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$?
questions.explain: $TM$ has dimension $2n$; for $n=3$ this is $6$.

questions.q: Under a chart change $y=y(x)$, a tangent vector with components $X^i$ becomes $\tilde X^j = (\partial y^j/\partial x^i)X^i$. If $X=(2,0)$ in coordinates $x$ and the Jacobian at the point is $\begin{pmatrix}3&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}$, what is $\tilde X^1$?
questions.explain: $\tilde X^1 = 3\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 0 = 6$.

hard.q: A smooth curve $\gamma:(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\to M$ defines a tangent vector $\gamma'(0)\in T_pM$ as a derivation on smooth functions. Which statement correctly orders the steps in showing this derivation satisfies the Leibniz rule?
choices: Compute $\gamma'(0)(fg)$ via the chain rule; apply the product rule for real functions; substitute $f(p)$ and $g(p)$
Verify $\gamma'(0)$ is linear first, then separately check Leibniz without using the chain rule
Assume Leibniz holds by definition of manifold, then verify linearity
Use the chart map to reduce to $\mathbb{R}^n$, then apply Leibniz for partial derivatives before pulling back
hard.explain: $\gamma'(0)(fg)=\frac{d}{dt}\big|_{t=0}(fg\circ\gamma)=f(p)\,g'(0)+g(p)\,f'(0)=f(p)\,\gamma'(0)(g)+g(p)\,\gamma'(0)(f)$ by the real product rule, confirming Leibniz directly without passing to a chart.

hard.q: Let $f:M\to N$ be a smooth map between manifolds of dimensions $3$ and $5$. What is the dimension of $\ker(df_p)$ if $df_p$ has rank $2$?
hard.explain: By the rank-nullity theorem applied to $df_p:T_pM\to T_{f(p)}N$, $\dim\ker(df_p)=\dim T_pM-\operatorname{rank}(df_p)=3-2=1$.

hard.q: Which is a correct counterexample showing that the tangent bundle $TS^2$ is not trivial (i.e., $TS^2\ncong S^2\times\mathbb{R}^2$)?
choices: The hairy ball theorem: every smooth vector field on $S^2$ must vanish somewhere, so no global frame exists
$S^2$ is compact, and compact manifolds never have trivial tangent bundles
A rank-$2$ bundle over a $2$-manifold is always non-trivial
$TS^2$ has the wrong Euler characteristic to be trivial
hard.explain: A trivial rank-$n$ bundle admits $n$ everywhere-linearly-independent global sections, i.e., a global frame. The hairy ball theorem asserts every tangent vector field on $S^2$ vanishes somewhere, so no non-vanishing global section exists, let alone a full frame. Hence $TS^2$ is non-trivial.

questions.q: The tangent bundle $TM$ of a smooth $n$-manifold $M$ is:
choices: a rank-$n$ real vector bundle over $M$; its total space is a smooth $2n$-manifold whose fibre over $p$ is $T_pM$
a subset of $M\times\mathbb{R}^n$ isomorphic to $M\times\mathbb{R}^n$ for every $M$
the dual of the cotangent bundle only when $M$ is orientable
questions.explain: $TM=\bigsqcup_{p\in M}T_pM$ is a rank-$n$ vector bundle over $M$; each fibre is an $n$-plane, and the total space is a smooth manifold of dimension $2n$. $TM$ is trivial ($\cong M\times\mathbb{R}^n$) iff $M$ is parallelizable — false in general (e.g. $S^2$).

questions.q: A smooth vector field on $M$ is:
choices: a smooth section of $TM$, i.e. a smooth map $X\colon M\to TM$ with $\pi\circ X=\mathrm{id}_M$
questions.explain: A vector field assigns to each point $p\in M$ a vector $X_p\in T_pM$ smoothly, i.e. a smooth section of the projection $\pi\colon TM\to M$. Equivalently, a derivation on $C^\infty(M)$.

questions.q: On $\mathbb{R}^2$, the vector field $X = x\,\partial_x + y\,\partial_y$ acts on the function $f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$ as $Xf$. What is $(Xf)(1,2)$?

hard.q: A smooth manifold $M$ is called parallelizable if $TM$ is trivial (isomorphic to $M\times\mathbb{R}^n$). Which list is correct?
choices: $S^1$, $S^3$, and $S^7$ are the only parallelizable spheres (a theorem of Adams / Bott–Milnor / Kervaire)
hard.explain: A classical theorem: $S^n$ is parallelizable iff $n\in\{1,3,7\}$, corresponding to the multiplicative structures on $\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H},\mathbb{O}$. The hairy-ball theorem handles $S^2$; more is needed for higher dimensions.

hard.q: For a smooth $n$-manifold $M$, what is the dimension of the total space $TM$ of its tangent bundle when $n=5$?
hard.explain: $\dim TM = 2n$. For $n=5$ this is $10$: a chart $(U,\varphi)$ on $M$ gives a chart on $TM$ with coordinates $(x^1,\dots,x^n,\dot x^1,\dots,\dot x^n)$.

hard.q: Let $X\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ act on $C^\infty(M)$ as a derivation. Which property is NOT required?
hard.explain: Derivations are $\mathbb{R}$-linear Leibniz operators, automatically local (locality follows from Leibniz applied to bump functions). Multiplicativity $X(fg)=X(f)X(g)$ is the defining property of a ring homomorphism, not a derivation; derivations detect infinitesimal deformations, homomorphisms detect point evaluations.

questions.q: A smooth map $f\colon M^m\to N^n$ is an immersion at $p$ iff $df_p$:
questions.explain: Immersion = injective differential, rank $m$. Submersion is the surjective case with rank $n$.

questions.q: By the regular value theorem, if $c$ is a regular value of $g\colon\mathbb{R}^{n+1}\to\mathbb{R}$ then $g^{-1}(c)$ is a smooth manifold of dimension $n$. For $g(x)=\|x\|^2$ on $\mathbb{R}^4$ and $c=1$, what is the dimension of $g^{-1}(1)=S^3$?
questions.explain: $g\colon\mathbb{R}^4\to\mathbb{R}$, and $1$ is regular, so $g^{-1}(1)$ has dimension $4-1=3$.

questions.q: Whitney's embedding theorem states that every smooth $n$-manifold admits a smooth embedding into:
questions.explain: Whitney (1944): every smooth $n$-manifold embeds smoothly in $\mathbb{R}^{2n}$, and immerses in $\mathbb{R}^{2n-1}$.

hard.q: The figure-eight $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $f(t)=(\sin 2t,\sin t)$, is an injective immersion but not an embedding. Which property fails?
choices: $f$ is not an immersion: $df_t$ is zero at some $t$
$f$ is not a topological embedding: the induced topology on $f(\mathbb{R})$ differs from the subspace topology of $\mathbb{R}^2$ near the crossing point
$f(\mathbb{R})$ is not a manifold of any dimension
hard.explain: $f$ is injective and $df_t$ is everywhere non-zero, so it is an injective immersion. However, the image is a figure-eight whose crossing point has a neighbourhood in $f(\mathbb{R})$ that is not homeomorphic to an interval: nearby points in the domain map to four branches meeting at the origin, whereas the subspace topology sees all four. Thus $f$ is not a homeomorphism onto its image and fails to be an embedding.

hard.q: Let $g:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^2$, $g(x,y,z)=(x^2+y^2+z^2,\, x+y+z)$. At the point $p=(1,0,0)$, what is the rank of $dg_p$?
hard.explain: $dg_p = \begin{pmatrix}2&0&0\\1&1&1\end{pmatrix}$. The first row has leading entry $2$ and the second has entry $1$ in the second column; the two rows are linearly independent, so $\operatorname{rank}(dg_p)=2$, making $p$ a regular point.

hard.q: By the constant-rank theorem, if $f:M^m\to N^n$ has constant rank $r$ near $p$, what is the local normal form of $f$?
hard.explain: The constant-rank theorem (local form): choosing adapted charts around $p$ and $f(p)$, $f$ looks like the standard projection–injection $(x^1,\dots,x^m)\mapsto(x^1,\dots,x^r,0,\dots,0)\in\mathbb{R}^n$. The first $r$ coordinates are kept; the rest are sent to zero.

questions.q: An integral curve of a smooth vector field $X\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ through $p$ is a smooth curve $\gamma\colon I\to M$ with $\gamma(0)=p$ satisfying:
questions.explain: An integral curve is a trajectory that follows the vector field: its velocity at every time equals the value of $X$ at the current position, i.e. $\gamma'(t)=X_{\gamma(t)}$.

questions.q: On $\mathbb{R}$, the vector field $X=x\,\partial_x$ has integral curve through $x_0=1$ given by $\gamma(t)=e^t$. What is $\gamma(\ln 3)$?
questions.explain: Solving $\dot x=x$ with $x(0)=1$ gives $x(t)=e^t$. Then $\gamma(\ln 3)=e^{\ln 3}=3$.

questions.q: On a compact smooth manifold $M$, every smooth vector field is:
choices: complete — its flow $\Phi^X_t$ is defined for all $t\in\mathbb{R}$
questions.explain: On a compact manifold integral curves cannot escape to infinity in finite time, so the maximal domain of each integral curve is all of $\mathbb{R}$. Equivalently, the flow $\Phi^X_t$ is defined for every real $t$ — $X$ is complete.

hard.q: Which is a correct counterexample to the claim 'every smooth vector field on a non-compact manifold is complete'?
choices: $X=\partial_x$ on $\mathbb{R}$ — its integral curves are straight lines and escape to infinity
$X=x^2\,\partial_x$ on $\mathbb{R}$ — the integral curve through $x_0=1$ is $\gamma(t)=1/(1-t)$, which blows up at $t=1$
$X=0$ on $\mathbb{R}$ — the zero field has no flow
$X=\partial_x+\partial_y$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ — its integral curves leave every compact set
hard.hint: Escape to infinity is fine; incompleteness needs blow-up in finite time.
hard.explain: The ODE $\dot x=x^2$, $x(0)=1$ has solution $x(t)=1/(1-t)$, which escapes to $+\infty$ as $t\to 1^-$. So the maximal interval of existence is $(-\infty,1)$ — strictly less than $\mathbb{R}$ — and $X=x^2\partial_x$ on $\mathbb{R}$ is incomplete. Leaving compact sets in finite time per se is fine (A, D) as long as the curve stays in $M$; only blow-up in finite time breaks completeness.

hard.q: The flow $\Phi^X_t$ of a complete vector field $X$ satisfies which identity?
choices: $\Phi^X_s\circ\Phi^X_t=\Phi^X_{s+t}$, i.e. the flow is a one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms
$\Phi^X_t$ is a linear map for every $t$
hard.explain: Uniqueness of ODE solutions, together with time-translation invariance, forces $\Phi^X_{s+t}=\Phi^X_s\circ\Phi^X_t$ wherever both sides are defined; when $X$ is complete this holds on all of $M\times\mathbb{R}^2$, making $\{\Phi^X_t\}_{t\in\mathbb{R}}$ a one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms of $M$.

hard.q: Let $X=-y\,\partial_x+x\,\partial_y$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ (rotation generator). Starting at $(1,0)$, the integral curve is $\gamma(t)=(\cos t,\sin t)$. What is the $y$-coordinate of $\Phi^X_{\pi/2}(1,0)$?
hard.explain: $\Phi^X_{\pi/2}(1,0)=(\cos(\pi/2),\sin(\pi/2))=(0,1)$, so the $y$-coordinate is $1$.

questions.q: The Lie bracket of two smooth vector fields $X,Y\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ is defined on functions by:
questions.explain: $[X,Y] = XY - YX$ as operators on $C^\infty(M)$. The second-order terms cancel and the commutator is again a derivation, hence a vector field.

questions.q: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $X=\partial_x$ and $Y=x\,\partial_y$, the Lie bracket is $[X,Y]=\partial_y$. Applying $[X,Y]$ to $f(x,y)=y^2$ at the point $(3,4)$ gives what number?
questions.explain: $[X,Y]f = \partial_y(y^2) = 2y$. At $(3,4)$ this evaluates to $2\cdot 4 = 8$.

questions.q: Select every identity that holds for the Lie bracket on $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ for arbitrary $X,Y,Z\in\mathfrak{X}(M)$ and $f\in C^\infty(M)$.
questions.hint: Antisymmetry + Jacobi makes $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ a Lie algebra; the bracket is $\mathbb{R}$-bilinear but not $C^\infty$-bilinear — pulling out a function picks up a derivation term.
questions.explain: Antisymmetry and Jacobi together make $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ a Lie algebra over $\mathbb{R}$. From antisymmetry, $[X,X]=0$ — so options (3) and (4) fail. The bracket is not $C^\infty$-bilinear: $[fX,Y]=f[X,Y]-Y(f)X$, with the extra $-Y(f)X$ term reflecting the fact that $Y$ differentiates the coefficient $f$. This is why the Lie bracket is a differential operator, not a tensor.

hard.q: The Lie bracket satisfies the Leibniz-like identity $[X,fY] = f[X,Y] + (Xf)Y$ for $f\in C^\infty(M)$. Which statement follows?
choices: The bracket is $\mathbb{R}$-bilinear but not $C^\infty(M)$-bilinear — the extra $(Xf)Y$ term obstructs $C^\infty$-linearity in the second slot
The bracket is zero for all $f$
The bracket depends on a choice of connection
hard.explain: The formula $[X,fY]=f[X,Y]+(Xf)Y$ shows a derivation-of-scalars correction term. Over $\mathbb{R}$ the bracket is bilinear, but over $C^\infty(M)$ it fails — unlike the pointwise operations of a vector bundle. The bracket is a genuinely differential (not tensorial) operation.

hard.q: Two smooth vector fields $X,Y$ on $M$ have flows that commute, $\Phi^X_s\circ\Phi^Y_t=\Phi^Y_t\circ\Phi^X_s$ for all small $s,t$, iff:
choices: $X$ and $Y$ are parallel at one point
hard.explain: This is the flow-commutativity theorem: the flows commute (on overlapping domains) iff the bracket $[X,Y]$ vanishes identically. The infinitesimal obstruction to flow commutativity is exactly the bracket, consistent with $\Phi^{-Y}_t\Phi^{-X}_t\Phi^Y_t\Phi^X_t(p)=p+t^2[X,Y]_p+O(t^3)$.

hard.q: On $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $X = \partial_x$ and $Y = x\,\partial_y + y\,\partial_x$, compute the $\partial_y$-component of $[X,Y]$ evaluated at any point.
hard.explain: For vector fields written as $X=X^i\partial_i$, $Y=Y^j\partial_j$, the bracket components are $[X,Y]^k = X^i\partial_i Y^k - Y^i\partial_i X^k$. Here $X=(1,0)$ and $Y=(y,x)$, with $X$ having constant coefficients. $[X,Y]^y = X^i\partial_i Y^y - Y^i\partial_i X^y = \partial_x(x) - 0 = 1$. So $[X,Y]^y = 1$ is constant (independent of position).

questions.q: A standard construction of a smooth partition of unity subordinate to an open cover $\{U_\alpha\}$ of a paracompact smooth manifold $M$ proceeds as follows. Pick the correct next step.
steps: Pass to a locally finite refinement $\{V_\beta\}$ of $\{U_\alpha\}$ — possible because $M$ is paracompact.

For each $V_\beta$, choose a smooth bump function $\psi_\beta\colon M\to[0,1]$ with $\operatorname{supp}\psi_\beta\subset V_\beta$ and $\psi_\beta>0$ on a smaller open set $W_\beta\subset V_\beta$ such that $\{W_\beta\}$ still covers $M$.
Form the locally finite sum $\Psi = \sum_\beta \psi_\beta$, which is smooth and strictly positive everywhere on $M$ since the $\{W_\beta\}$ cover $M$.
choices: Define $\rho_\beta = \psi_\beta / \Psi$; then each $\rho_\beta$ is smooth with $\operatorname{supp}\rho_\beta\subset V_\beta$, $0\le\rho_\beta\le 1$, and $\sum_\beta\rho_\beta = 1$.
Define $\rho_\beta = \psi_\beta$; the locally finite sum being everywhere positive already gives $\sum_\beta\psi_\beta = 1$.
Replace each $\psi_\beta$ by the characteristic function $\mathbf{1}_{V_\beta}$ to ensure $\sum = 1$.
Use a metric to define $\rho_\beta$ as the distance to the complement of $V_\beta$.
questions.hint: Normalize each bump by the locally finite (smooth, positive) sum.
questions.explain: The final step is normalization: $\rho_\beta := \psi_\beta/\Psi$ is smooth (denominator is smooth and positive), satisfies $\operatorname{supp}\rho_\beta\subset V_\beta$, and $\sum_\beta\rho_\beta = \Psi/\Psi = 1$. Local finiteness of $\{V_\beta\}$ guarantees the pointwise sum has only finitely many nonzero terms. The result is a smooth partition of unity subordinate to the (refined) cover.

questions.q: On which class of smooth manifolds is the existence of smooth partitions of unity guaranteed?
choices: every paracompact smooth manifold (in particular, every second-countable Hausdorff smooth manifold)
questions.hint: Smooth partitions of unity exist on any paracompact smooth manifold; our definition already assumes second countability + Hausdorff, which imply paracompactness, so every smooth manifold in this…
questions.explain: Smooth partitions of unity exist on any paracompact smooth manifold; our definition already assumes second countability + Hausdorff, which imply paracompactness, so every smooth manifold in this notebook admits them. Compactness and orientability are irrelevant.

questions.q: Suppose $\{\rho_1,\rho_2,\rho_3\}$ is a partition of unity on $M$ and at a point $p$, $\rho_1(p)=0.2$, $\rho_2(p)=0.5$. What is $\rho_3(p)$?
questions.explain: By definition $\rho_1(p)+\rho_2(p)+\rho_3(p)=1$, so $\rho_3(p)=1-0.2-0.5=0.3$.

hard.q: Which is the standard use of a partition of unity in the proof that every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric?
choices: Choose local Riemannian metrics $g_\alpha$ in each chart; glue via $g=\sum_\alpha\rho_\alpha g_\alpha$, which is a positive-definite tensor because a convex combination of positive-definite forms is positive-definite
Use $\rho_\alpha$ to extend a single chart's Euclidean metric to all of $M$
Deduce existence from Whitney embedding, bypassing partitions of unity entirely
Average the metric over the Lie group of diffeomorphisms of $M$
hard.explain: On each chart domain $U_\alpha$ pull back the Euclidean metric to get a local Riemannian metric $g_\alpha$. A convex combination $\sum_\alpha\rho_\alpha g_\alpha$ of positive-definite symmetric forms with non-negative weights summing to $1$ is itself positive-definite wherever the sum is non-empty, which is everywhere because $\sum\rho_\alpha=1$. Smoothness follows from local finiteness.

hard.q: Why does the analogous gluing fail for holomorphic (complex-analytic) structures — i.e. why is there no holomorphic partition of unity on a complex manifold?
choices: Because holomorphic functions satisfy the maximum principle and so a non-constant compactly supported holomorphic function cannot exist on a connected open set
Because complex manifolds are never paracompact
Because bump functions do exist holomorphically but their sum need not equal $1$
Because complex manifolds fail to be Hausdorff
hard.explain: Smooth bumps exist because $C^\infty$ is extremely flexible — non-analytic functions like $e^{-1/x^2}$ can vanish on open sets. Holomorphic functions obey the identity theorem / maximum principle, so a non-zero holomorphic function with compact support would have to be zero on an open set and hence identically zero. This rigidity kills holomorphic partitions of unity and is why sheaf cohomology and \v{C}ech methods replace them on complex manifolds.

questions.q: A smooth manifold $M$ is orientable iff:
choices: $M$ admits a smooth atlas whose every transition map has positive Jacobian determinant
questions.hint: Orientability is equivalent to the existence of an oriented atlas — one for which every transition $\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1}$ has $\det D(\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1})>0$ on its…
questions.explain: Orientability is equivalent to the existence of an oriented atlas — one for which every transition $\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1}$ has $\det D(\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1})>0$ on its domain. Equivalently, $M$ admits a nowhere-vanishing top-degree form.

questions.q: Which of the following manifolds is NOT orientable?
questions.explain: The Möbius band has a single side: going once around the core circle and returning flips a local frame's handedness, so no consistent orientation exists. $S^2$, $T^2$, and $\mathbb{RP}^3$ are orientable ($\mathbb{RP}^n$ is orientable iff $n$ is odd).

questions.q: How many orientations does a connected orientable smooth manifold admit?
questions.explain: An orientation is a choice of sign on the top exterior power at each point, varying continuously. On a connected manifold there are exactly two global choices, differing by an overall sign flip.

hard.q: Which statement correctly identifies $\mathbb{RP}^n$'s orientability pattern?
hard.explain: $\mathbb{RP}^n = S^n/\{\pm 1\}$. The antipodal map $a\colon S^n\to S^n$ is orientation-preserving iff its degree $(-1)^{n+1}$ is $+1$, i.e. iff $n$ is odd. So the quotient is orientable precisely when $n$ is odd. In particular $\mathbb{RP}^1\cong S^1$ and $\mathbb{RP}^3\cong\mathrm{SO}(3)$ are orientable; $\mathbb{RP}^2$ and $\mathbb{RP}^4$ are not.

hard.q: On the 2-sphere $S^2$ with the usual orientation, consider the two stereographic charts from $N$ and $S$. On their overlap $S^2\setminus\{N,S\}$, the transition is $\varphi_S\circ\varphi_N^{-1}(u,v)=(u,v)/(u^2+v^2)$. What is the sign of the Jacobian determinant of this transition at $(u,v)=(1,0)$? Enter $+1$ or $-1$.
hard.explain: Write the transition as $(u,v)\mapsto(u/r^2,v/r^2)$ with $r^2=u^2+v^2$. Its Jacobian at $(1,0)$ has determinant $-1/r^4=-1$. So these two stereographic charts are oppositely oriented; to get a positively oriented atlas one composes $\varphi_S$ with a reflection. This is why the two-chart stereographic atlas is a smooth atlas but not an oriented atlas as stated — an adjustment is needed.

hard.q: Which of the following is equivalent to the manifold $M$ being orientable?
hard.explain: The standard equivalence: $M$ is orientable iff $\Lambda^n T^*M$ has a nowhere-zero smooth section, i.e. there is a volume form. Every smooth manifold admits a Riemannian metric (via partitions of unity), so (B) is not equivalent. Trivial $TM$ (parallelizability) is strictly stronger than orientability — e.g. $S^2$ is orientable but not parallelizable.
